Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes Amirhossein Taghipour a, Pooria Karami a,*, Aria Khalili a,b

, Maral Bagheri Kaloo a

, Behzad Ahvazi a,c, Aman Ullah c

, Jae-Young Cho b , Mohtada Sadrzadeh a,** a Department of Mechanical Engineering, 10-241 Donadeo Innovation Center for Engineering, Advanced Water Research Lab (AWRL), University of Alberta, Edmonton, AB, Canada, T6G 1H9b Nanotechnology Research Centre, National Research Council Canada, 11421 Saskatchewan Drive, Edmonton, AB, Canada, T6G 2M9c Department of Agricultural, Food and Nutritional Science, Lipids/Materials Chemistry Group (LMCG), 360C South Academic Building, University of Alberta, Edmonton, AB, Canada, T6G 2P5

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:Thin film composite membranesPolyesterLigninDepolymerizationInterfacial polymerization

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: A B S T R A C T Membrane technology remains essential for water treatment and desalination. While polyamide thin-film composite (TFC) membranes dominate the industry, their susceptibility to fouling reduces efficiency and shortens operational lifespan. Advanced chemical modification strategies have been developed to overcome this challenge, aiming to improve membrane performance and durability. Polyester-based TFC membranes offer a promising alternative, providing a negatively charged surface. However, their lower salt rejection than poly┬¡amide membranes remains a key limitation. In this study, we investigated the application of depolymerized lignin, a naturally abundant biopolymer, as a phenolic monomer for fabricating polyester TFC membranes. The native lignin has a much larger molecular size compared to conventional monomers, which limits its diffusion and reactivity during membrane formation. We utilized a microwave-assisted depolymerization technique to fractionate lignin into smaller moieties, resulting in oligomeric/monomeric lignin with a lower molecular mass and higher reactive sites. This approach enabled faster membrane fabrication and significantly improved membrane performance. The membranes fabricated by depolymerized lignin exhibited substantially improved salt rejection, achieving up to 98.8 % for sodium sulfate and 54 % for sodium chloride removal.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: depolymerized lignin exhibited substantially improved salt rejection, achieving up to 98.8 % for sodium sulfate and 54 % for sodium chloride removal. The developed membranes exhibited excellent antifouling properties, as demonstrated by sodium alginate fouling tests, with a flux recovery ratio of over 85 %. This research presents an innovative approach to enhancing non-polyamide TFC membranes, opening up new possibilities for eco-friendly and efficient membrane technologies in practical desalination applications.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content:
1. Introduction The growing demand for clean water has increased the importance of advanced water treatment technologies [1]. Among these, membrane-based filtration processes (e.g., RO, NF, UF, etc.) are widely used due to their effectiveness in removing a broad range of contami┬¡nants from bacteria and viruses to divalent and monovalent salts, while offering high water recovery. Among these, for desalination purposes, the current processes primarily rely on thin-film composite (TFC) membranes due to their high separation efficiency and flexibility in design [1]. These membranes are produced through interfacial polymerization (IP), where monomers react at the interface of two immiscible phases, forming a thin, selective layer on a porous support [2,3]. Interfacial polymerization offers multiple tools for optimizing TFC membrane performance through adjusting the ratio of reactants [4], additives [5,6], process conditions [7,8], and exploring new monomers [9,10]. Nanofiltration (NF) membranes have gained significant atten┬¡tion because of their unique ability to selectively reject multivalent ions and organic compounds [11]. This selective separation makes NF membranes especially useful for water softening [12], removing organic contaminants [13], wastewater treatment [14], and certain industrial applications.

* Corresponding author.** Corresponding author.E-mail addresses: pkarami@ualberta.ca (P. Karami), sadrzade@ualberta.ca (M. Sadrzadeh).

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: Most NF TFC membranes are fabricated using petrochemical-based monomers, such as piperazine (PIP) [15]. Polyamide membranes syn┬¡thesized by PIP are designed to balance permeability and selectivity for diverse applications; however, they still encounter challenges, such as fouling, which significantly affect their lifespan and operational ex┬¡penses [16,17]. Moreover, environmental concerns associated with petroleum-based materials highlight the importance of developing non-polyamide TFC membranes using more sustainable alternatives [18,19]. Specifically, hydroxyl-containing materials have been explored as alternatives to conventional amine-based monomers, enabling a transi┬¡tion from polyamide to polyester membrane chemistry. Zhang et al. developed a novel polyesteramide (PEA) NF membrane through inter┬¡facial polymerization of serinol and trimesoyl chloride [20]. The opti┬¡mized membrane exhibited high hydrophilicity or fouling resistance, a molecular weight cut-off of 474 Da, and water permeability of 6.0 LMH bar-1, with salt rejection rates of Na2SO4 (96.27 %) > MgSO4 (83.92 %) > NaCl (58.68 %) > MgCl2 (28.76 %). While polyester TFC membranes offer better antifouling performance than conventional polyamide TFCs, their lower salt rejection, due to a less crosslinked selective layer, re┬¡mains a critical limitation. Various strategies have been explored to address this. Elimelech et al.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: on, due to a less crosslinked selective layer, re┬¡mains a critical limitation. Various strategies have been explored to address this. Elimelech et al. [21] fabricated PEA TFC membranes via layer-by-layer interfacial polymerization of 3,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid, PIP, and trimesoyl chloride (TMC), achieving up to 99.1 % NaCl rejec┬¡tion rate and water permeability of 3 LMH bar-1. However, membranes fabricated without PIP still exhibited lower performance compared to conventional polyamide TFCs. Shen et al. [22] utilized thermal-facilitated interfacial polymerization (TFIP) with glucose as the aqueous monomer, demonstrating that higher temperatures enhanced monomer diffusion and cross-linking, yielding polyester membranes with rejection rates of>99 % for Na2SO4. In another study, glucose and piperazine were used as aqueous-phase monomers to fabricate poly┬¡ester, polyamide, and PEA TFC membranes to investigate the role of monomer ratio and pH on performance [23]. PEA membranes contain┬¡ing 10 wt% glucose achieved 99.2 % Na2SO4 rejection with high flux (82.5 LMH), while pure polyamide membranes showed similar rejection (99.5 %) but lower flux (56 LMH). Polyester membranes exhibited the highest flux (173 LMH) but poor rejection (58 %). Despite significantly lower PIP consumption (0.1 wt%) compared to glucose (10 wt%), the results confirmed that achieving high salt rejection still critically de┬¡pends on PIP.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: wer PIP consumption (0.1 wt%) compared to glucose (10 wt%), the results confirmed that achieving high salt rejection still critically de┬¡pends on PIP. Lignin, a complex biopolymer abundant in plant cell walls, is a byproduct of the pulp and paper and bioethanol industries, with growing applications in biofuels, bioplastics, and adhesives [24]. In recent years, lignin has gained attention in the fabrication of TFC membranes, primarily as an additive to enhance polyamide membrane performance through bulk incorporation or surface modification. Due to its multiple hydroxyl groups, lignin can enable the formation of polyester-based selective layers. Shamaei et al. [25] first demonstrated the use of sulfur-rich kraft lignin (SKL) to fabricate TFC membranes for forward osmosis, reporting a twofold increase in water flux (33.5 LMH) compared to unmodified membranes, alongside reduced fouling and improved wettability. Similarly, lignin incorporation in TFC nano┬¡filtration membranes enhanced negative surface charge and antifouling resistance [26]. Other types of lignin, such as lignin alkali, have also been explored. Zhang et al. [27] used this lignin as an additive in the aqueous PIP solution during the IP reaction. With its hydrophobic aro┬¡matic rings and hydrophilic functional groups, lignin alkali acted as a surfactant, reducing interfacial tension and regulating PIP distribution.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: aro┬¡matic rings and hydrophilic functional groups, lignin alkali acted as a surfactant, reducing interfacial tension and regulating PIP distribution. This promoted faster monomer diffusion, enabling the formation of highly crosslinked membranes with enhanced water permeability and ion separation performance. Despite these advances, purely lignin-based polyester TFC membranes, without additional modifications, have yet to achieve salt rejection rates comparable to conventional polyamide membranes. New strategies are required to improve the divalent salt rejection of lignin-based polyester TFC membranes. Several factors contribute to the low rejection performance of these membranes. First, the slower reac┬¡tion (esterification) kinetics of lignin with its different classes of hy┬¡droxyl groups contrast with the rapid amine-acyl chloride reactions typically used to form polyamide layers [28]. Second, ligninÔÇÖs high molecular weight leads to increased free volume in the polymer matrix, reducing salt rejection [26]. Third, its large molecular size restricts diffusion to the reaction interface, further hindering polymer formation [29]. Therefore, accelerating polymerization and improving the mem┬¡braneÔÇÖs structural integrity are essential for effective water purification. Reducing the molecular size of lignin can address these challenges.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: e mem┬¡braneÔÇÖs structural integrity are essential for effective water purification. Reducing the molecular size of lignin can address these challenges. Smaller molecular species exhibit higher diffusivity, facilitating faster transport through boundary layers, thereby improving the polymeriza┬¡tion rate at the interface and creating a more compact, selective layer. Depolymerization techniques have been widely explored to reduce the molecular size of polymers, such as lignin. Methods such as thermal treatment, catalytic depolymerization [30,31], enzymatic breakdown [32], and ionic liquid depolymerization have been investigated [33]. Among them, microwave-assisted depolymerization has proven partic┬¡ularly effective, offering rapid and selective bond cleavage at lower temperatures and shorter reaction times compared to conventional heating [34ÔÇô36]. Lignin primarily consists of aromatic units linked by CÔÇôO and CÔÇôC bonds, with the ╬▓-O-4 ether linkage being the most abundant and most susceptible to cleavage. The ╬▓-O-4 ether linkage connects the ╬▓-carbon of one lignin unit and the 4-position (para-┬¡position) of the aromatic ring of another. The initial scission of ╬▓-O-4 bonds typically dominates the depolymerization process, facilitating the opening of the lignin structure [37]. In this study, we utilized microwave-assisted depolymerization to break down softwood kraft lignin (SKL), enhancing its diffusion and reactivity during interfacial polymerization.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: crowave-assisted depolymerization to break down softwood kraft lignin (SKL), enhancing its diffusion and reactivity during interfacial polymerization. We systematically evalu┬¡ated the effect of depolymerized lignin on the morphology, salt rejec┬¡tion, water permeability, and antifouling properties of the resulting TFC membranes. This work introduces a novel approach to improving ligninbased polyester NF membranes, advancing them toward practical water treatment applications. 2. Materials and methods 2.1. Materials The polyethersulfone (PES) support layer was produced using a rollto-roll casting machine. Dimethylformamide (DMF) solvent, PES, and the additives (polyethylene glycol (PEG) and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) were sourced from Sigma-Aldrich. Sodium chloride (NaCl), so┬¡dium sulfate (Na2SO4), methylene blue, and methyl orange were pur┬¡chased from Sigma-Aldrich. West Fraser Timber Co. Ltd., located in Hinton, AB, Canada, supplied softwood kraft lignin (SKL). Piperazine (PIP), trimesoyl chloride (TMC, 98 %), and n-hexane were obtained from Fisher Scientific. Sodium alginate (SA) was purchased from SigmaAldrich and utilized as a negatively charged organic foulant in the fouling experiments. All chemicals and solvents were of reagent grade and used as received without further purification. A Ubbelohde viscometer, purchased from Fisher Scientific, was utilized for viscosity measurement. 2.2.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: used as received without further purification. A Ubbelohde viscometer, purchased from Fisher Scientific, was utilized for viscosity measurement. 2.2. Fabrication of PES support layer The nonsolvent-induced phase separation (NIPS) method was used to fabricate the PES support. PES (the primary polymer), PVP, and PEG were dissolved in DMF solvent to prepare the polymeric solution. The solution was stirred at 100 rpm at 50 ÔùªC for 24 h. It was then degassed in a vacuum oven for 15 min to remove air bubbles. PES membranes were prepared by casting the solution onto a non-woven polyester fabric using a film applicator. The casting speed was set at 20 mm/s, with the gap (the distance between the blade and the fabric) set at 110 ╬╝m. Finally, the cast film was immersed in a coagulation bath containing deionized

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: A. Taghipour et al. Journal of Membrane Science 734 (2025) 124512 water and left overnight to ensure complete solvent removal from the polymeric support. 2.3. Depolymerization of lignin The microwave oven used in this study was a domestic Homestyles (BA-7560-WH) with ten power settings, ranging from 90 W to 900 W. A mid-range power setting was chosen for the experiments. The micro┬¡wave frequency and exposure time were adjusted to avoid boiling the solution and prevent changes in the monomer concentrations. SKL so┬¡lutions were prepared in distilled water at a concentration of 5 wt%. The solutions were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min to remove insoluble particles and impurities before microwave treatment, then stored at room temperature. 50 mL of the solution were transferred into a 500 ml beaker and microwaved at 50 % power (~450 W) for 1, 3, and 5 min. The solutions were then labeled as L1 (no depolymerization), L2 (1 min depolymerization), L3 (3 min depolymerization), and L4 (5 min depo┬¡lymerization). Once the solution cooled to ambient temperature, the volume of the solution was measured using a graduated cylinder to determine the amount of evaporated water. The evaporated volume of distilled water was subsequently added, after which the viscosity of SKL solutions and the hydrodynamic size of SKL were measured. 2.4.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: ated volume of distilled water was subsequently added, after which the viscosity of SKL solutions and the hydrodynamic size of SKL were measured. 2.4. Fabrication of polyester TFC membranes The lignin-based polyester membranes were synthesized through interfacial polymerization between lignin in the aqueous phase (water) and TMC in the organic phase (hexane). Fig. 1 illustrates the depoly┬¡merization of SKL and the fabrication steps of the TFC membranes. First, the PES support was saturated with 5 wt% lignin solutions for 5 min. The solution was removed with an acrylic roller. Next, a 0.4 wt% TMC so┬¡lution was poured onto the support to initiate the polymerization for 1 min. The membrane was then cured in an oven at 60 ÔùªC for 5 min. The synthesized membranes were labeled as follows: M1 (control membrane with no depolymerization), M2 (1-min depolymerization), M3 (3-min depolymerization), and M4 (5-min depolymerization). The synthesized polyester TFC membranes were stored in Milli-Q water at room tem┬¡perature before further characterization. 2.5. Lignin solution characterization To analyze the lignin solutions before and after depolymerization, we conducted two types of characterization. First, viscosity measure┬¡ment experiments were performed using a Ubbelohde viscometer at 20 ÔùªC following ASTM D445. The Ubbelohde viscometer measures the time required for a liquid to flow through a calibrated capillary tube under the force of gravity.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: ng ASTM D445. The Ubbelohde viscometer measures the time required for a liquid to flow through a calibrated capillary tube under the force of gravity. This time, referred to as the flow time (t), is directly related to the liquidÔÇÖs kinematic viscosity. Specifically, the flow time refers to the period during which the liquidÔÇÖs meniscus passes be┬¡tween two marked points on the viscometer. The specific viscosity was calculated based on flow time measurements for each lignin solution by varying the concentrations. First, the relative viscosity (╬Àrel) was determined as follows: ╬Àrel = tt0

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: By plotting the reduced viscosity vs. lignin solution concentration (c), the intrinsic viscosity (╬À) was determined by extrapolating the reduced viscosity (╬Àsp/c) to zero concentration, extending the fitted line to the Y-axis. Following the determination of intrinsic viscosity, the viscosityaverage molecular weight (Mw) for each lignin sample was calculated using the Mark-Houwink equation. The Mark-Houwink equation de┬¡scribes an empirical relationship between a polymerÔÇÖs intrinsic viscosity (╬À) and its molecular weight (M), expressed as: ╬À = KÔïàMa(4) This relation was originally developed independently by Herman Mark and Roelof Houwink [38] and is widely used to determine polymer molecular weights from viscometry data. In this equation, (K) and (a) are polymer-solvent specific constants that depend on factors such as polymer structure, solvent, and temperature. Physically, the exponent (a) reflects the polymerÔÇÖs hydrodynamic behavior (e.g. coil flexibility or rigidity) in solution, while (K) is a proportionality factor related to the polymer-solvent interaction. In this work, first, the Mark-Houwink constants were determined experimentally using two lignin solutions with known molecular weight for the lignin/solvent system.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: first, the Mark-Houwink constants were determined experimentally using two lignin solutions with known molecular weight for the lignin/solvent system. The intrinsic viscosities of these reference lignin samples were measured with the Ubbelohde viscometer and plotted as log[╬À] versus log M to generate a Mark-Houwink calibration line; the slope of this line provided the exponent (a), and the intercept yielded (K). Once these constants were found, the viscosity-average molecular weight of each unknown lignin sample was obtained by putting its measured intrinsic viscosity into the MarkÔÇôHouwink equation and solving for M. In practice, this meant rearranging Mark-Houwink equation as follows: M =(╬À k )1a(5) Using this approach, the molecular weight of each lignin sample was calculated from its intrinsic viscosity, with the Mark-Houwink parame┬¡ters, and reported for the lignin samples before and after depolymer┬¡ization. To ensure the accuracy of the reported results, the measurements were repeated at least five times, and the molecular weight numbers were reported as a range. The hydrodynamic size and the size distribution of depolymerized lignin were also assessed through dynamic light scattering (DLS, ALVCGS-3 Goniometer) at a scattering angle of 90Ôùª. Prior to measurement, lignin solutions were diluted to 0.02 w% using ultrapure water. Each DLS experiment was conducted three times with separately prepared samples to guarantee accurate and reproducible results.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: 2.6. Membrane characterization Several techniques were used to characterize the synthesized mem┬¡branes. Field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Zeiss Sigma 300 VP) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips/FEI Morgagni 268) were used to study the structural and morphological changes of the TFC layer. The roughness and thickness of the TFC membranes were measured using a Veeco Dimension 3100 atomic force microscope (AFM). The measurements employed AFM tips with a cur┬¡vature radius of ~8 nm and a cantilever spring constant of 5 N/m. Roughness analysis was performed in tapping mode, using an amplitude setpoint below 0.6 V and a scan rate of 0.4 Hz, and the data were pro┬¡cessed using Nanoscope software. The chemical composition of the membranes was determined with K-Alpha XPS (Thermofisher Scientific, E. Grinstead, UK), and attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy. ATR-FTIR spectra were collected at room temperature using an Agilent Cary 600 spectrometer, covering the 400-4000 cm-1 range with a resolution of 4 cm-1. Zeta potential tests were conducted to measure the surface charge of the fabricated membranes using a SurPASS Electrokinetic Analyzer (Anton Paar, Austria) within a pH range of 4ÔÇô9. The pH of the testing solutions was adjusted using sodium hydroxide or hydrochloric acid. Each zeta po┬¡tential measurement was repeated three times, and the average value was reported.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: s adjusted using sodium hydroxide or hydrochloric acid. Each zeta po┬¡tential measurement was repeated three times, and the average value was reported. To qualitatively evaluate the surface charge of the fabri┬¡cated TFC membranes under practical conditions, we conducted a dye adsorption test using oppositely charged dye molecules. Small coupons of the fabricated membranes (2 cm * 5 cm) were soaked in 13.5 mL of 10 ppm dye solutions: methylene blue (a cationic dye) and methyl or┬¡ange (an anionic dye). After 12 h of soaking at room temperature, the dye concentrations in the solutions were measured using UVÔÇôVis spec┬¡troscopy, and the amount of dye adsorbed onto the membrane surface was calculated in ╬╝g/cm2. This approach was not intended as a replacement for standard zeta potential measurements, but rather as a practical and visual confirmation of surface charge trends observed in the electrokinetic analysis. Similar dye-binding techniques have been employed in the literature to probe membrane surface charge and functional groups, showing good correlation with zeta potential results [39]. The wettability of the membranes was evaluated with contact angle measurement using the sessile drop method with a Kruss DSA 100 in┬¡strument (Germany). A 2 ╬╝L droplet of deionized water was placed on the membrane surface, and the contact angle was measured 10 times for each sample.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: strument (Germany). A 2 ╬╝L droplet of deionized water was placed on the membrane surface, and the contact angle was measured 10 times for each sample. Also, the molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of the syn┬¡thesized TFC membranes was determined through a series of experi┬¡ments using 250 ppm aqueous solutions of polyethylene glycol (PEG) with molecular weights ranging from 200 to 1000 Da (200, 300, 400, 600, 1000). MWCO, defined as the lowest molecular weight of a solute that the membrane can reject with at least 90 % efficiency, was measured using a cross-flow filtration setup. PEG solutions with increasing molecular weights were filtered sequentially, starting from the lowest molecular weight. During each filtration run, samples were collected from both the feed and permeate, and the total organic carbon (TOC) content was measured using a TOC analyzer to calculate the rejection rate. 2.7. Filtration tests A cross-flow filtration system was utilized to evaluate the perfor┬¡mance of the developed TFC membranes. The membrane cell (Sterlitech CF016) has an active surface area of 20.6 cm2. The feed flow rate was set at 2 ┬▒ 0.05 L/min, and the transmembrane pressure was maintained at 50 ┬▒ 2 psi. We kept the feed solution temperature stable at 25 ┬▒ 3 ÔùªC using a Fisher Scientific Isotemp 3013 water bath.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: ne pressure was maintained at 50 ┬▒ 2 psi. We kept the feed solution temperature stable at 25 ┬▒ 3 ÔùªC using a Fisher Scientific Isotemp 3013 water bath. To have better ac┬¡curacy in measuring permeate flux, we employed two methods: (a) a digital flow meter was used to facilitate calculating flux based on the membraneÔÇÖs surface area, and (b) the permeate collection technique, which involved weighing the collected water on a digital balance. Pure water flux was monitored for 2 h at 50 psi. Then, the salt solution was added to the feed tank. Salt rejection (R) was calculated using the following equation: R =[Cf -Cp Cf ]* 100(6) Cp and Cf are salt concentrations in the permeate and feed solutions.Fouling tests were performed using SA (150 ppm) as the foulant. The initial pure water flux (Jwi) was measured for 30 min. Then, the foulant solution was added to the feed, and the membrane flux (Js) was moni┬¡tored continuously for 5 h. Then, the membrane was rinsed with pure water at a high cross-flow rate for 30 min to remove residual foulants, and the pure water flux (Jwf) was measured again. The flux recovery ratio (FRR) and flux decline (FD) were calculated as follows: FRR = Jwf Jwi

The procedure was repeated for four cycles, with all cycles starting at the same initial flux (Jwi), which was set by adjusting the pressure.

2.8. Stability and durability tests To assess how the membranes perform under more realistic condi┬¡tions, long-term water exposure tests and chlorine stability tests were performed. Large thin-film composite (TFC) membrane sheets of the control membrane (M1) and the optimum membrane (based on filtra┬¡tion results) were prepared. We followed two different methodologies. 1. For the study of membrane durability in water, four small membrane coupons were immersed in water. Every three days, one membrane was taken from each beaker and analyzed for water flux and Na2SO4 rejection.2. For the chlorine stability tests, four small membrane coupons were immersed in a 200 ppm chlorine solution (sodium hypochlorite). Different membranes were tested every 24 h. To enable better comparison, flux and rejection values were normalized by their initial values (day 0) and reported as normalized flux and normalized rejection.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Effect of the depolymerization process on SKL macromolecule We utilized two methods to investigate the effect of depolymeriza┬¡tion on SKL macromolecules. First, the viscosity of SKL solutions with different depolymerization times was measured to assess the impact of chain breaking. Specifically, intrinsic viscosity (╬À) was determined, as it provides valuable insight into changes in polymer molecular size and chain structure. Intrinsic viscosity reflects the polymerÔÇÖs contribution to the solution viscosity. It represents the specific viscosity per unit con┬¡centration at the limit of zero polymer concentration, minimizing the influence of polymer-polymer interactions. Lower molecular weight lignin reduces chain entanglement, leading to lower viscosity in lignin solutions [40]. The intrinsic viscosity of the lignin solutions is plotted in Fig. 2(a). The intrinsic viscosity decreased from 0.0595 dL/g for the pristine solution (L1) to 0.0467, 0.0278, and 0.0075 dL/g for L2, L3, and L4 samples, respectively, showing the effectiveness of the microwave depolymerization technique. To further quantify the depolymerization effect, the viscosityaverage molecular weights of the lignin samples were calculated from the intrinsic viscosity data using the MarkÔÇôHouwink equation. Fig. 2(c) shows the results in a bubble plot, where each bubble represents the average molecular weight and its corresponding distribution range. The

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: Fig. 2. (a) Intrinsic viscosity, (b) Hydrodynamic size distribution of the pristine (L1) and depolymerized (L2, L3, and L4) lignin solutions, and (c) viscosity average molecular weight of the lignin solutions. A. Taghipour et al. Journal of Membrane Science 734 (2025) 124512 calculated average molecular weights decreased progressively with increasing depolymerization time, from approximately 100,000 g/mol for L1 to 50,000, 9,000, and 800 g/mol for L2, L3, and L4, respectively. The sharp reduction in molecular weight supports the trend seen in the intrinsic viscosity results and confirms the effectiveness of the microwave-assisted depolymerization technique. Second, the hydrodynamic size and distribution of depolymerized lignin were assessed through dynamic light scattering. The hydrody┬¡namic size distribution (Fig. 2(b)) revealed the emergence of more and smaller peaks in the depolymerized lignin solutions, further supporting the idea that lignin is broken down into smaller chains. 3.2. Effect of SKL depolymerization on molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) Fig. 3 presents the MWCO values of all synthesized membranes, where the MWCO is defined as the molecular weight of PEG at which each membrane achieves 90 % rejection. The MWCO decreased from 957 Da for the pristine membrane (M1) to 332 Da for M4. The impact of depolymerization is more substantial after 3 min (M3), as the MWCO decreased from 660 Da for M2 to 354 Da for M3.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: (M1) to 332 Da for M4. The impact of depolymerization is more substantial after 3 min (M3), as the MWCO decreased from 660 Da for M2 to 354 Da for M3. Shorter lignin chains generated from depolymerization likely contribute to forming a more tightly packed polyester structure, reducing the pore size of the nano┬¡filtration membranes. This structural change can potentially enhance the overall separation performance by producing a polyamide layer with less free volume within its matrix. 3.3. Morphological characterization of synthesized polyester TFC membranes The surface characteristics of TFC membranes can be influenced by various factors, such as the chemistry of the polymeric layer (functional groups), substrate, and reaction conditions (temperature, pH, etc.) [41]. 3D AFM images, contact angle measurements, and top-surface SEM images are presented in Fig. 4. There is a noticeable difference between the structural features of the pristine TFC membrane (M1) and the

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: Fig. 4. From top to bottom, AFM, contact angle, and top section SEM images of the fabricated TFC membranes. A. Taghipour et al. Journal of Membrane Science 734 (2025) 124512 membranes fabricated with depolymerized lignin (M2-4). The rough┬¡ness values exhibited a clear ascending trend from M2 to M3 and M4, with M4 displaying the highest roughness values. These observations can be attributed to the increased diffusion of depolymerized lignin to the interface, leading to a higher rate of the polycondensation reaction and, correspondingly, a rougher surface [42]. Contact angle results revealed an upward trend with increasing depolymerization time, which can be explained by the changes in sur┬¡face hydrophilicity [41]. The contact angle increased from 71.5Ôùªfor M1 to 88.5Ôùªfor M4. The hydrophilicity of the poly (SKL-TMC) membranes originates from carboxylic groups formed by the hydrolysis of unreacted COCl groups from the TMC monomer and hydroxyl groups from unreacted functional groups of SKL. For M3 and M4, smaller lignin molecules can diffuse more freely to the water/heptane interface and participate more effectively in the reaction. As a result, a greater portion of SKL is involved in the polymerization, reducing the number of re┬¡sidual carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the membrane surface, which leads to a less hydrophilic surface. Therefore, the surface is expected to be less hydrophilic with fewer unreacted hydrophilic groups.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: surface, which leads to a less hydrophilic surface. Therefore, the surface is expected to be less hydrophilic with fewer unreacted hydrophilic groups. The top-surface SEM images of the membrane revealed distinct dif┬¡ferences between the fabricated membranes. The top surface of polyester-based TFC nanofiltration membranes commonly exhibits a spherical-like (nodular) morphology [23]. In this study, lignin was pre-treated using depolymerization to enhance its diffusion to the interface. SEM images of the fabricated membranes revealed that longer depolymerization times resulted in more pronounced spherical surface features, which can be attributed to the improved mobility and diffusion of smaller lignin fragments during the polymerization process. To sup┬¡port this observation, image processing techniques were employed to quantify the nodular structures, indicating an increase in nodular con┬¡centration from 1.5 % (of total area) for the pristine membrane (M1) to around 7.5 % for the fabricated membrane with lignin depolymerized for 5 min (M4). Cross-sectional TEM images (Fig. 5) were used to evaluate the effect of lignin depolymerization on the TFC layer thickness. The pristine membrane (M1) exhibited the thinnest layer (~70 nm), whereas M2, prepared with depolymerized lignin, showed an increased thickness of approximately 155 nm. Similar cross-sectional TEM results were observed for M3 and M4.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: red with depolymerized lignin, showed an increased thickness of approximately 155 nm. Similar cross-sectional TEM results were observed for M3 and M4. This trend suggests that extended depoly┬¡merization yields smaller lignin fragments, which more readily diffuse to the reaction interface, resulting in a thicker polyamide layer within the same reaction time. 3.4. Surface charges of synthesized polyester TFC membranes The surface charge of TFC membranes is a critical property that significantly affects their antifouling performance and, consequently, their service life [43,44]. Fig. 6(a) depicts the zeta potential of the Fig. 5. Cross-section TEM images of the fabricated TFC membranes.

content: A. Taghipour et al. Journal of Membrane Science 734 (2025) 124512 fabricated membranes. All lignin membranes exhibited negative surface charges, with the surface charge becoming less negative as the depoly┬¡merization time increased. The dye adsorption experiments were conducted by immersing membrane coupons in dye solutions and measuring the dye concentra┬¡tion before and after immersion to determine the amount adsorbed onto the membrane surfaces. The results of dye adsorption experiments are shown in Fig. 6(b) (methylene blue) and Fig. 6(c) (methyl orange). These results align with the zeta potential experiments, as the adsorption of the positively charged methylene blue displayed a decreasing trend from M1 to M4. In contrast, the adsorption trend of the negatively charged methyl orange did not exhibit a significant change, likely due to the negative surface charge of all the fabricated membranes. A negative surface charge is a characteristic of poly(SKL-TMC) membranes [26,45]. This characteristic mainly results from the depro┬¡tonation of residual carboxylic groups formed by the hydrolysis of unreacted acyl chloride groups (TMC monomers) and the presence of sulfur groups in SKL. Phenolic hydroxyl groups of SKL are also present but are not expected to significantly contribute to the surface charge under the tested conditions (pKa of phenolic ÔÇôOH is typically around 9ÔÇô10).

Taghipour et al. Journal of Membrane Science 734 (2025) 124512 reactive sites. As a result, fewer unreacted negatively charged functional groups, such as carboxylic groups, will remain in the final structure, leading to a lower negative charge on the surface (M4). 3.5. Chemical composition of the synthesized polyester TFC membranes FTIR and XPS analysis (Fig. 7) was conducted to confirm the suc┬¡cessful formation of the polyester layer. Fig. 7(a) shows the general survey and high-resolution C1s spectra for all synthesized membranes. The survey spectra confirm the presence of carbon (285.5 eV), oxygen (532 eV), sulfur (165 eV), and sodium (1070 eV). Table 1 presents the peak area ratios corresponding to various carbon chemical bonds. The high-resolution C1s spectra of the polyester membranes were deconvo┬¡luted, revealing distinct peaks at 285.0 eV (CÔÇôC/CÔÇôH), 286.2 eV (CÔÇôCOOH), and 289.1 eV (OÔÇôCÔÇôÔÇôO). The OÔÇôCÔÇôÔÇôO peak corresponds to the presence of free carboxyl groups and ester bonds. As the depoly┬¡merization time increased, the ratio of ester linkages rose from 7.59 % for M1 to 10.6 % for M4. This suggests that shorter lignin chains pro┬¡duced during depolymerization more effectively participate in interfa┬¡cial polymerization, resulting in a greater formation of ester linkages. Fig.

content: ro┬¡duced during depolymerization more effectively participate in interfa┬¡cial polymerization, resulting in a greater formation of ester linkages. Fig. 7(b) displays the FTIR results, with the characteristic absorption band near 1730 cm-1 indicating the ester carbonyl stretching vibration (CÔÇôÔÇôO), confirming the successful formation of polyester linkages in the TFC selective layer. Additionally, sulfonic acid groups ( -SO3H) in Kraft lignin, are typically introduced through side reactions involving sulfur species, mainly sodium sulfide (Na2S), during the Kraft pulping [46], which increases ligninÔÇÖs water solubility and charge density. The emergence of a peak at approximately 1010 cm-1, associated with the symmetric stretching vibrations of sulfonate groups (SÔÇôÔÇôO), confirms the incorporation of SKL into the TFC layer. In the right panel of Fig. 7(b), the increased intensity of ester characteristic peaks (at 1730 cm-1) from

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: M1 to M4 indicates a higher number of ester linkages formed. This also supports our hypothesis that shorter SKL molecules diffuse more effec┬¡tively into the reaction zone, forming a greater polyester mass over the substrate. 3.6. Separation performance Fig. 8(a) shows the water flux, NaCl rejection, and Na2SO4 rejection of the synthesized polyester TFC membranes (M1ÔÇôM4). There is a noticeable reduction in water flux as the lignin depolymerization time increases. The water flux decreased from 65 LMH for M1 to 19 LMH for M4. However, the salt rejection results show a clear improvement with increased lignin depolymerization time. The rejection rates of NaCl and Na2SO4 increased from 16 % to 86 % for the pristine membrane (M1) to 54 % and 98.8 % for M4. Fig. 8(b) presents the long-term filtration of membranes, showing salt rejection over 12 h of filtration. All mem┬¡branes showed consistent rejection performance with minimal fluctua┬¡tions for both salts, indicating excellent stability and resistance to performance deterioration during prolonged filtration. The observed trend in the permselectivity of the synthesized poly┬¡ester thin-film composite (TFC) membranes can be attributed to a combination of size exclusion (steric hindrance) and Donnan exclusion (electrostatic repulsion) mechanisms.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: ite (TFC) membranes can be attributed to a combination of size exclusion (steric hindrance) and Donnan exclusion (electrostatic repulsion) mechanisms. The significant decrease in water flux, along with increased rejection rates, particularly for sulfate (a divalent ion), indicates the formation of a denser, more compact poly┬¡ester network with smaller pore sizes. The decrease in permeability aligns with the previously observed drop in molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) values and surface hydrophilicity. This change is attributed to the enhanced diffusion of smaller lignin chains and the formation of a more compact polyester structure with reduced free volume and decreased pore connectivity. The increased compactness enhances the molecular sieving capability of membranes made from depolymerized lignin, effectively restricting the passage of larger ions and molecules. Moreover, the increased negative surface charge of the modified membranes (from M2 to M3) significantly enhances salt rejection through electrostatic repulsion, or Donnan exclusion. Specifically, membranes with greater negative charges will strongly repel negatively charged species, such as sulfate ions [47], leading to a high rejection rate of Na2SO4, up to 98.8 % in M4 in this work. Therefore, the combined effects of decreased pore size (steric exclusion) and increased surface negativity (electrostatic repulsion) are the dominant mechanisms

3.7. Antifouling properties The antifouling properties of membranes fabricated with pristine lignin (M1) and the most extensively depolymerized lignin (M4) were evaluated using sodium alginate (SA) as a model foulant over four consecutive 5-h fouling cycles (Fig. 9). Fig. 9(a) shows the normalized water flux (J/J0) during the fouling experiment. Both membranes exhibited a comparable flux decline in each cycle, with M4 showing a slightly greater reduction. Fig. 9(b) presents the Flux Recovery Ratio (FRR) and Flux Decline (FD) of M1 and M4 membranes over the filtra┬¡tion cycles. M1 demonstrated slightly higher FRR and lower FD values compared to M4 in all cycles.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content:
As shown by the zeta potential (Fig. 6) and membrane wettability (Fig. 4) results, increasing the depolymerization time led to a less negative surface charge and reduced hydrophilicity of the membranes. These changes can potentially increase membrane susceptibility to fouling by sodium alginate, a negatively charged foulant, due to weak┬¡ened electrostatic repulsion and reduced surface hydration. Addition┬¡ally, the lower hydrophilicity may facilitate the accumulation of organic foulants on the membrane surface by lowering the hydration layer, which typically acts as a protective barrier. Although depolymerization reduced the hydrophilicity and negative charge of the membrane surface, the overall antifouling performance of M4 remained comparable to that of M1 without a significant decline. This is because M4 is still a highly negative membrane comparable to most typical polyamide nanofiltration membranes, which often possess even less negative or slightly positive surface charges [23,48]. content: A. Taghipour et al. Journal of Membrane Science 734 (2025) 124512 mentioning that M4 exhibits significantly better separation performance than M1, with higher salt rejection. Table 2 summarizes recent advances in using lignin for fabricating TFC membranes. Karami et al. employed sulfur-rich kraft lignin to fabricate TFC membranes [26]. Using 7 wt% lignin in aqueous solution along with 0.2 wt% terephthaloyl chloride in heptane with 4 4-min re┬¡action time, they achieved a water permeance of 18 LMH bar-1 and rejections of 92.2 and 43 % rejection of Na2SO4 and NaCl, respectively. In another study, loose NF membranes were synthesized by LbL self-assembly of polyethylenimine and sodium lignosulfonate, followed by glutaraldehyde crosslinking, yielding 4 LMH bar-1 water permeance and Na2SO4 and NaCl rejection of 69 % and 39 %. Sodium lignosulfonate was also utilized with silver nanoparticles to fabricate thin-film nano┬¡composite membranes [49]. This combination facilitated the uniform dispersion of silver nanoparticles, leading to the formation of nano┬¡channels in the membraneÔÇÖs active layer and enhancing its surface hy┬¡drophilicity and negative charge. The optimal membrane exhibited a water permeance of 40 LMH bar-1 and rejections of 22 % and 70 % for NaCl and Na2SO4, respectively. Lignin modification strategies have also enhanced the performance of lignin-based membranes.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: tions of 22 % and 70 % for NaCl and Na2SO4, respectively. Lignin modification strategies have also enhanced the performance of lignin-based membranes. Aminated mes┬¡oporous silica nanospheres incorporated into lignin-based membranes enhanced water permeance (49.9 LMH bar-1) and fouling resistance, albeit with low NaCl rejection (16.4 %) [50]. In another study, NF membranes fabricated using aminated alkaline lignin and piperazine as aqueous co-monomers achieved high water permeance (32.7 LMH bar-1) and strong divalent salt rejection (96.8 % for Na2SO4 and 91.0 % for MgSO4). Here, lignin contributed to a thinner, more hydrophilic selective layer with stable long-term operation [51]. Lignin alkali derived from paper pulp waste was utilized as an aqueous-phase addi┬¡tive to regulate the IP reaction during the fabrication of polyamide TFC-NF membrane [27]. It promoted the diffusion and partitioning of the aqueous-phase monomer (PIP) into the organic phase, accelerating the IP reaction and enhancing interfacial instability. The optimal membrane demonstrated a permeance of 26 LMH bar-1 and an excellent salt rejection rate (99 % for Na2SO4 and 30 % for NaCl). Other lignin forms have also been used to modify conventional TFC-RO membrane recipes. Lignin nanoparticles, prepared via wet milling, were incorpo┬¡rated into the polyamide selective layer of TFC membranes through spray-assisted multilayer interfacial polymerization [52].

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: wet milling, were incorpo┬¡rated into the polyamide selective layer of TFC membranes through spray-assisted multilayer interfacial polymerization [52]. Incorporating lignin nanoparticles enhanced membrane surface properties and filtra┬¡tion performance by increasing hydrophilicity, surface roughness, and negative surface charge. Fabricated membranes (based on the number of layers) exhibited high water permeability (up to 32.7 LMH bar-1) and moderate NaCl salt rejection (84ÔÇô91 %). In our recent study on lignin-based membranes, a novel surface coating technique was devel┬¡oped to modify TFC-NF membranes [45]. Aqueous lignin and organic TMC in heptane solutions were sprayed onto the prefabricated poly┬¡amide TFC layer through a spray-assisted interfacial polymerization technique. This formed a smooth, spot-like polyester selective layer with enhanced negative surface charges due to residual carboxyl groups from lignin and TMC. The polyester-coated membrane exhibited improved antifouling performance, with an FRR of 90 %, and a 15 % higher fouling

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: Fig. 10. Results of the membrane durability and chlorine resistance tests for membranes M1 (a and c) and M4 (b and d). A. Taghipour et al. Journal of Membrane Science 734 (2025) 124512 resistance than uncoated membranes during Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) filtration tests. Nonetheless, it remains challenging for polyester TFC membranes, including lignin-based TFC types, to achieve dye and salt rejection rates comparable to those of tight polyamide NF mem┬¡branes, which restricts their mass production and industrial applica┬¡tions. To our knowledge, this research has presented the highest reported rejection values in the literature utilizing sulfonated kraft lignin alone without any additives, achieved with such a short IP reac┬¡tion time (1 min).

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: 3.8. Stability and durability assessment Besides their excellent antifouling properties, polyester thin-film composite (TFC) membranes are also known for their superior chlo┬¡rine stability compared to polyamide-based membranes, due to the absence of chlorine-sensitive amide bonds [48]. Unlike polyamide structures, the ester linkages in polyester membranes are inherently more resistant to chlorine-induced oxidative degradation [53,54]. Fig. 10 depicts the results of the extended filtration tests as well as the chlorine resistance experiments. The normalized flux and salt (Na2SO4) rejection results (compared to the initial results) showed a stable trend even after 12 days, which shows the stability of the fabricated TFC membranes. Regarding the chlorine stability results, a slight decrease in rejection and an increase in flux were observed over a four-day testing period, indicating the high chlorine resistance of the lignin-based polyester TFC membranes. 4. Conclusion This research presents a novel approach for fabricating nonpolyamide nanofiltration membranes with remarkable separation per┬¡formance comparable to conventional polyamide membranes. The microwave-assisted depolymerization technique effectively breaks down lignin into smaller units, creating an increased number of reactive sites.

citation.title: Lignin depolymerization: A sustainable strategy to enhance the separation performance of biopolymeric polyester membranes

content: . The microwave-assisted depolymerization technique effectively breaks down lignin into smaller units, creating an increased number of reactive sites. This process enables smaller molecules to diffuse to the reaction interface more rapidly and efficiently, thereby significantly enhancing reaction kinetics and improving the quality of the resulting polymer layer. In this study, we successfully reduced the reaction time to just 1 min. Such a short reaction time dramatically enhances the practicality and efficiency of membrane production, particularly for industrial-scale fabrication of TFC membranes. The membranes developed in this study exhibited significant and stable enhancements in salt rejection, achieving rates of up to 98.8 % for sodium sulfate and 54 % for sodium chloride, among the highest reported values for purely lignin-based polyester membranes, while maintaining excellent antifouling proper┬¡ties. The fabricated TFC membranes with depolymerized lignin exhibi┬¡ted a lower surface charge as the depolymerization time increased, due to the reaction of more functional groups. The more neutral surface is safer when dealing with feedwater that contains a mixture of both negatively and positively charged foulants. This research presents an effective alternative to traditional petroleum-based membranes, pro┬¡moting sustainability in membrane technology.
